note: This is the most contested area. Fiscal-impact estimates depend heavily on method (snapshot vs lifetime), assumptions about return migration, and which spending is attributed to migrants. Across academic studies the net fiscal impact, positive or negative, is consistently within about plus or minus 1% of GDP. Asylum cost figures are firmer (National Audit Office). Figures here should always be presented with their source and method, not as settled facts.

metrics.metric_name: Government spending on the asylum system (Home Office plus Ministry of Justice)
metrics.source_name: National Audit Office, An analysis of the asylum system
metrics.notes: Home Office plus Ministry of Justice only. The NAO states this does not capture all costs, particularly those incurred by local authorities, so it understates the full system cost. Most recent authoritative figure (published 10 December 2025).

metrics.metric_name: Home Office spending on asylum accommodation and support
metrics.source_name: National Audit Office, An analysis of the asylum system
metrics.notes: Largest single area of Home Office asylum spend; a subset of the £4.9bn total. Includes hotels, dispersal accommodation, cash support and local-authority dispersal grants.

metrics.metric_name: Home Office spending on asylum hotel accommodation
metrics.source_name: National Audit Office, An analysis of the asylum system
metrics.notes: Within the £3.4bn accommodation total; roughly £5.77m per day. Down from about £3bn in 2023-24. Hotels were about 76% of the annual cost of asylum accommodation contracts but housed only about 35% of people in asylum accommodation.

metrics.metric_name: Average cost of an asylum hotel place per person per night
metrics.source_name: National Audit Office, The Home Office's asylum accommodation contracts (HC 874, 2024-25)
metrics.notes: Compares with about £20 per night for dispersal (non-hotel) accommodation. Shows why hotels dominate cost despite housing a minority of people.

metrics.metric_name: Asylum accommodation contracts — revised 10-year cost projection
metrics.source_name: National Audit Office, The Home Office's asylum accommodation contracts (HC 874, 2024-25)
metrics.notes: Lifetime cost of the three contracts now projected at £15.3bn, more than triple the original estimate of about £4.5bn. This is a projection over the contract life, NOT an annual figure.

metrics.metric_name: Illustrative lifetime net fiscal contribution of a migrant (arriving at 25, average earnings)
metrics.source_name: Office for Budget Responsibility, Fiscal Risks and Sustainability (September 2024), via Migration Observatory
metrics.notes: A STYLISED SCENARIO, not an observed outcome. Applies to one hypothetical migrant arriving at 25 on average earnings, living to 80. The same OBR analysis finds low-wage migrants have a NEGATIVE lifetime impact and high-wage migrants a larger positive one; the result is highly sensitive to age, earnings and children. Do not present as a typical or average figure.

metrics.metric_name: Net fiscal impact of immigration as a share of GDP (range across studies)
metrics.unit: % of GDP (less than plus or minus 1%)
metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory, The Fiscal Impact of Immigration in the UK
metrics.notes: Method-dependent and contested. Across multiple studies the net impact (positive or negative) is consistently less than plus or minus 1% of GDP. Dynamic (lifetime) studies tend more positive; static (snapshot) studies more mixed. No single correct figure exists.

metrics.source_name: ONS Labour Force Survey, via Migration Observatory labour market briefing
metrics.notes: EU-born employment rate has exceeded UK-born and non-EU since about 2006. ONS Labour Force Survey has known reliability and sample-size limitations in recent years; treat as provisional and cross-check against the primary release.

metrics.source_name: ONS Labour Force Survey, via Migration Observatory
metrics.notes: Non-EU-born rate was about 75.1% in the same period. The aggregate comparison masks a gender split: migrant men have higher employment than UK-born men, but migrant women have lower employment than UK-born women.

metrics.source_name: Migration Observatory, Migrants in the UK labour market: an overview
metrics.notes: About 5.9 million of UK employees were adult migrants (roughly a fifth of the workforce). Definitions of 'migrant' vary (foreign-born vs non-UK nationality) by dataset.

metrics.metric_name: Non-British nationals as a share of NHS staff (England, Hospital and Community Health Service)
metrics.source_name: House of Commons Library, NHS staff from overseas: statistics (CBP-7783)
metrics.notes: About 325,000 of about 1.5 million staff reported a non-British nationality. Higher for doctors (about 36%) and nurses (about 30%). England only; the exact share depends on whether 'unknown' nationalities are included in the denominator.

metrics.metric_name: Non-British nationals as a share of the adult social care workforce (England)
metrics.source_name: Skills for Care, The state of the adult social care sector and workforce in England 2025
metrics.notes: Approximate; non-EU share has risen from about 10% to about 25% since 2021-22. Some summaries cite about 19% non-UK depending on year and definition; treat the exact figure as approximate. Overseas arrivals to care roles fell from about 105,000 (2023-24) to about 50,000 (2024-25).

metrics.source_name: House of Commons Library, UK immigration fees (CBP-9859)
metrics.notes: The surcharge rose 66% in February 2024 (standard rate £624 to £1,035 per year), estimated to raise about £1.1bn extra per year. Period basis should be confirmed against the primary briefing.

metrics.metric_name: Home Office visa and immigration fee income
metrics.source_name: House of Commons Library, UK immigration fees (CBP-9859)
metrics.notes: Separate from the Immigration Health Surcharge (about £2.6bn) and the employer Immigration Skills Charge (about £0.6bn), so total immigration-related revenue exceeds about £6bn.

metrics.metric_name: MAC recommended Skilled Worker general salary threshold
metrics.source_name: Migration Advisory Committee, Annual Report 2025 and review of salary requirements
metrics.notes: The MAC recommends keeping the threshold at £41,700, finding this maximises fiscal benefits. It found that raising it to £52,500 would cut net migration by only about 2,900 to 5,100 a year but cost public finances about £520m to £710m — illustrating that cutting migration via salary thresholds can be fiscally counter-productive because fiscal gains concentrate among the highest earners.
